studyMetadata.title: Form Elements Demo
studyMetadata.description: A demo of form elements in reVISit.

response.prompt:
# Form Element

 This page shows many of the form elements that are supported in reVISit.

## This is a level 2 text header

 ### This is a level 3 subheader

 You can use text fields with markdown headings to structure your survey. You can make it **bold** or *italic*. You can also add links like [this](https://revisit.dev). Markdown also works in question prompts and secondary text.

response.secondaryText: A numeric example that is required to complete and has an option to say 'Don't know'.
response.infoText: This numerical input accepts values between 0 and 100.
response.placeholder: Enter your age here, range from 0 - 100

response.secondaryText: A slider for 0-100 with 3 breakpoints for bad, mid, and good
response.infoText: Use the slider to indicate your rating on a scale from 'Bad' to 'Good'.

response.secondaryText: A short text example
response.infoText: Your response can be a few words or a short sentence.

response.secondaryText: A text box for long text
response.infoText: Your response can be a detailed explanation or multiple sentences.

response.prompt: Dropdown example – which chart do you like best?
response.secondaryText: You can specify secondary text to clarify your question.
response.infoText: Select the chart type you prefer from the dropdown menu.

options.label: Bar
options.infoText: A bar chart is useful for comparing values across categories.

response.prompt: Multiselect dropdown example - which chart have you used before?
response.secondaryText: Dropdown for multi-selection
response.infoText: Select the chart types you have used before. You have to select at least 2 options and up to 3 options.

options.label: Line
options.infoText: A line chart is commonly used for trends over time.

response.prompt: Checkbox example, but this time with a long title that makes a ton of sense and is really cool
response.secondaryText: Checkboxes for multi-selection
response.infoText: Select your top 2 choices from the available options.

response.prompt: Horizontal Checkbox example, but this time with a long title that makes a ton of sense and is really cool
response.secondaryText: Checkboxes for multi-selection
response.infoText: Select your top 2 choices from the available options.

response.secondaryText: Radio buttons for single selection
response.infoText: Select the option that best represents your choice.

options.label: Option 1
options.infoText: This is info text for Option 1.

response.secondaryText: Radio buttons for single selection
response.infoText: Select the option that best represents your choice.
options: Option 2 this is long a long option that will wrap

response.secondaryText: Buttons that function as radio buttons, but look like buttons. Control with the arrow keys.
response.infoText: Choose the option that best represents your answer.

options.label: Option 1
options.infoText: This is info text for button option 1.

response.prompt:
## Likert Scales and Matrix Form Elements

 Matrix form elements allow you to ask a series of related questions that share the same set of answer options. This is useful for Likert scales, satisfaction ratings, and categorization tasks.

response.secondaryText: A Likert example with 9 items, also showing how to add a divider after the element.
response.infoText: This is a 9-point Likert scale from 'Dislike' to 'Like'. Select the number that best represents your opinion.

response.prompt: Rate your satisfaction from 1 (Highly Unsatisfied) to 5 (Highly Satisfied) for the following items.
response.secondaryText: A simple matrix of Likert responses with predefined options for satisfaction.
response.infoText: This is a matrix radio where you rate each item on a 5 point scale from 'Highly Unsatisfied' to 'Highly Satisfied'.

response.prompt: Which categories do the following items belong to?
response.secondaryText: A matrix of checkboxes with custom options for categorization.
response.infoText: For each animal, select all the categories that apply. You can select multiple checkboxes per row.

answerOptions.label: Has Legs
answerOptions.infoText: Use this when the animal has legs.

answerOptions.label: Has Wings
answerOptions.infoText: Use this when the animal has wings.

answerOptions.label: Can Swim
answerOptions.infoText: Use this when the animal can swim.

response.prompt:
# Default Values Demo

This page is a local test for response defaults. Every input below should render prefilled without participant interaction.

response.default: Prefilled long text value for local testing.

response.prompt: Multiselect dropdown default (string input normalized to array)
Checkbox default with Don't know option
response.secondaryText: This includes the I don't know checkbox for local testing.

response.prompt:
# Default Values - Optional Invalid Defaults

This page intentionally pre-fills optional responses with invalid values so they should show orange warnings instead of red required errors.

response.secondaryText: This is optional, but the default value is outside the allowed range.

response.prompt: Optional multiselect dropdown with too few defaults
response.secondaryText: This is optional, but the default selection violates the minimum selection rule.

response.prompt: Optional radio with a requiredValue mismatch
response.secondaryText: This is optional, but the prefilled answer intentionally does not match the configured requiredValue.

response.prompt: Optional checkbox with a requiredValue mismatch
response.secondaryText: This is optional, but the default selection intentionally does not match the requiredValue.

response.prompt:
# Default Values - Optional Mixed Valid And Invalid

This page mixes optional responses that start invalid because of their defaults with optional responses left blank, which should remain clean.

response.secondaryText: This should show an orange invalid state because the default is outside the allowed range.
This starts blank and required, so it should show the red required state after validation is revealed.

response.prompt: Optional multiselect dropdown with invalid default
response.secondaryText: This should show an orange invalid state because the default misses the minimum selection count.
This starts blank and required, so it should show the red required state after validation is revealed.
This should show an orange invalid state because the default does not match requiredValue.
This is optional and blank, so it should not be counted as invalid.

response.prompt:
# Custom Response

This page demonstrates a `custom` response implemented with React code from `src/public`. The widget below writes a structured JSON value into a single response id and contributes its own validation.
Pick a chart type and set your confidence.
response.secondaryText: The answer is stored as an object with `chartType`, `confidence`, and `rationale`.

response.prompt:
# Randomizing responses within a form element. 

 This shows how to randomize the order of questions inside a matrix response and how to randomize the order of options for buttons, checkboxes and radio questions.

response.secondaryText: The order of questions is randomized
The order of questions is randomized
The order of options is randomized
The order of options is randomized
The order of options is randomized
The order of options is randomized
The order of options is randomized

response.prompt:
# Randomizing Questions in a Form

 This shows how to randomize the order of questions in the form. Notice how the number before each question is different from the order number specified in the text. Note that currently the title is not randomized; you can exclude a form element from being randomized if you need to.

# Ranking Widgets

 This page shows the three types of ranking widgets available in reVISit: sublist ranking, categorical ranking, and pairwise ranking.
Rank your top 2 favorite fruits from the list below.
Sort these hobbies into the categories of HIGH, MEDIUM, and LOW based on your level of interest.

# Form Elements in Sidebars 

 This page shows form elements that can be used in the sidebar. 

 For all elements that have horizontal and vertical variants, you should always use the vertical one in the sidebars. 

 Matrix form elements are not supported in the sidebar. 

 Limit Likert scales to 7 or less to fit. 

 Limit the number of buttons to avoid overflow.

## Likert Scales and Matrix Form Elements

response.prompt:
# Unanswered Highlights - Mixed Layout

This page keeps Next enabled. Clicking it should reveal unanswered required responses across `aboveStimulus`, `belowStimulus`, and `sidebar` together.

# Unanswered Highlights - Grouped Inputs

Grouped responses should use the same warning size and spacing while still revealing only after a blocked Next attempt.

# Unanswered Highlights - Sidebar Only With Main Next

The required response is only in the sidebar, but the Next button stays in the main column.

# Unanswered Highlights - Above Stimulus Only With Main Next

The only required response lives above the stimulus area, while the Next button stays in the main column below.

response.secondaryText: This should still block Next and reveal its error state even though no required response is below the stimulus.

response.prompt:
# Unanswered Highlights - Styled Form Mix

This page borrows the `demo-style` form stylesheet and mixes required and optional responses across locations.
Optional comment in the main column

response.secondaryText: This should not count toward the unanswered summary.

response.prompt:
# Unanswered Highlights - Optional Only

All responses on this page are optional, so Next should continue without showing summary or inline error states.

# Unanswered Highlights - Website / Iframe Validation Mix

This page uses a `website` stimulus and keeps the required responses outside the stimulus frame to verify the summary and inline errors still appear correctly.

# Stimulus Validation - Forced Video Only

Next should remain disabled until the video finishes, without showing unanswered form warnings.

# Stimulus Validation - Forced Video With Form Elements

The video keeps Next disabled at first. After completion, Next becomes enabled and reveals unanswered form fields if needed.

parameters.title: Stimulus validation with a React component
parameters.description: This mock stimulus calls setAnswer and keeps Next disabled until you click Complete stimulus.

response.prompt:
# Training Validation - Check Answer Required

This page keeps Next disabled until the participant checks the answer and satisfies the training rule.

# Timer Validation - Next Button Enable Time

Next should be truly disabled for three seconds and then enable without any required response.

# Styled Validation — Neon Card

This page uses a heavily styled container (gradient background, neon border, shadow). Our validation highlighting should still show correctly: try clicking Next without filling in fields to see red required errors, and enter an out-of-range number to see an orange invalid error once errors are revealed.

response.secondaryText: Leave blank and hit Next to see the red required highlight.
The default is intentionally out of range; after clicking Next it should show an orange invalid error.

response.prompt:
# Styled Validation — External Stylesheet

This page uses `stylesheetPath` instead of inline `style`. The dark gradient card, hover lift on each question, and custom input colors come from the CSS file. Click Next without filling the required fields to verify red highlights still appear, and watch the numeric field flag orange once errors are revealed.

response.secondaryText: Default is out of range — orange invalid should appear after Next.

response.prompt:
# THE DAILY QUESTIONNAIRE

*An editorial on styled validation — proving that our red, orange, and sticky highlights cohabit gracefully with bespoke typography.*

response.secondaryText: Required — leaving this blank after Next should highlight it red.

response.prompt: Preferred sections (optional, must pick at least 2 if any)
response.secondaryText: Default selects only one; our orange invalid highlight should appear once Next is clicked.
